description: Dialogue between Prince Arjuna and Lord Krishna on duty, self-realization, and devotion.

chapters.name: Arjuna Vishada Yoga
chapters.summary: Arjuna experiences intense grief, despondency, and anxiety on seeing his relatives, uncles, cousins, and brothers on the battlefield of Kurukshetra. He refuses to fight them. Addresses family conflict, disputes with brothers/cousins, feeling unloved or betrayed by relatives, grief over family relations, moral crises, and fear of hurting family members.

chapters.name: Sankhya Yoga
chapters.summary: Krishna teaches the eternity of the Soul, overcoming grief and fear of death, performing selfless action (Karma Yoga), mind control, rising above emotional hurt, and traits of a person of steady wisdom. Addresses overthinking, exam stress, anxiety about results, feeling overwhelmed, bereavement, loss of loved ones, and relationship detachment.

chapters.name: Karma Yoga
chapters.summary: The path of selfless action, explaining why work is necessary, how to perform duty without desire for results, and action without expectation. Addresses career stress, lack of motivation, feeling burnt out by work, exam preparation, and finding meaning in everyday tasks.

chapters.name: Jnana Karma Sanyasa Yoga
chapters.summary: The Yoga of Knowledge and sacrifice, divine birth of Krishna, renunciation of action, and the fire of knowledge. Addresses spiritual ignorance, searching for truth, and understanding divine cycles.

chapters.name: Karma Sanyasa Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of renunciation, comparing renunciation of action with performance of selfless action, equanimity, and inner peace. Addresses mental peace, desire control, and emotional stability.

chapters.name: Dhyana Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of Meditation, controlling the restless and wandering mind, self-discipline, practice of moderation, and self-realization. Addresses overthinking, restlessness, stress relief, focus issues, loneliness, depression, and mental health.

chapters.name: Jnana Vijnana Yoga
chapters.summary: Knowledge of the Absolute, divine energy, and the four types of devotees. Addresses seeking God, finding faith, and understanding the universe.

chapters.name: Akshara Brahma Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of the imperishable Brahman, path to liberation, and remembrance of the Divine at the time of death. Addresses fear of death, mortality, afterlife, and spiritual consistency.

chapters.name: Raja Vidya Raja Guhya Yoga
chapters.summary: The sovereign science and secret, divine protection, and path of total devotion. Addresses seeking safety, feeling unprotected, building faith, and divine love.

chapters.name: Vibhuti Vistara Yoga
chapters.summary: The infinite divine opulences of God manifested in the creation. Addresses seeing divinity in nature, appreciation, and cosmic beauty.

chapters.name: Viswarupa Darshana Yoga
chapters.summary: Manifestation of the cosmic form of Krishna to Arjuna on the battlefield. Addresses fear, awe, and realizing the grand scale of existence.

chapters.name: Bhakti Yoga
chapters.summary: The path of devotion, characteristics of a dear devotee, personal vs impersonal worship, tolerance, and compassion. Addresses relationship hurt, handling unrequited love, dealing with feeling unloved or rejected by family/friends, cultivating compassion, and forgiveness.

chapters.name: Kshetra Kshetrajna Vibhaga Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of the field and the knower of the field (matter and spirit, body and soul). Addresses body image issues, health anxiety, and separating identity from the physical body.

chapters.name: Gunatraya Vibhaga Yoga
chapters.summary: The three modes of material nature (Sattva/purity, Rajas/passion, Tamas/ignorance) and transcending them. Addresses anger, laziness, lethargy, procrastination, greed, and toxic environments.

chapters.name: Purushottama Yoga
chapters.summary: Yoga of the supreme person, comparing the cosmic tree of creation and transcending the perishable. Addresses feeling disconnected, seeking roots, and finding freedom.

chapters.name: Moksha Sanyasa Yoga

description: Profound Advaita dialogue on non-duality and absolute freedom.

The passage moves from “Arriving with Laksmana at the lake filled with lotuses, lilies and fishes, Rama, his senses oppressed, burst into tears.” through “Abounding in lotuses, birds and beasts and vicious serpents moving about, it looks splendid indeed.” through “Various forest trees shaken by the force of the wind scatter flowers on the beautiful rocky surface of earth.”

Themes: mortality and immortality. Themes: devotion, freedom from attachment. Themes: devotion.
